summary: Fournier challenges the engineering belief in meritocracy, arguing that flat organizations without titles don't create fairness but instead enable shadow hierarchies that exclude outsiders. Rather than eliminating structure, leaders should explicitly define what each level requires and make those expectations transparent to everyone. She advocates for promotion committees composed of senior engineers rather than manager-driven promotions, which creates peer accountability and buy-in. The post acknowledges this approach requires significant work but argues that clarity in expectations ultimately builds better organizations.
key_points: Flat organizations without titles don't create meritocracy—they create shadow hierarchies that favor insiders and alienate diverse perspectives
True meritocracy requires explicit, clearly articulated expectations for each level, shared transparently with the entire team
If someone can 'lawyerball' their way to promotion using your criteria, either your criteria are incomplete or your bias is blocking deserved recognition
Promotion committees of senior engineers remove manager discretion and create peer-driven accountability for advancement
Meritocracy doesn't mean only rewarding the naturally gifted—it means creating clear paths for people who work hard to grow
money_quotes: Engineers like to believe in the idea of meritocracy. We want to believe that the best idea wins, that the person who produces the most value is rewarded, that we judge only on what a person brings to the table and nothing more.
Removing titles and pretending that the hierarchy doesn't exist does exactly the opposite of creating a meritocracy.
It most often creates a self-reinforcing system where shadow hierarchies rule the day and those outside the in-group have even less opportunity to see their ideas come to life.
'Meritocracy' doesn't mean 'Only reward people who are naturally gifted at what I value.' That's the thing I'm trying to stop doing!
If a person truly is meeting everything I have laid out as being necessary for working at a level, why would I not want to promote them to that level?
Creating a culture where ideas are welcome from anywhere requires cultivation with or without titles.
Promoting someone is not a gift bestowed by management but an acknowledgement by a group of peers that one should join their ranks.
key_insight: Eliminating formal structure doesn't create fairness—it just makes the real power dynamics invisible; true meritocracy requires explicit expectations and transparent processes.
